title: Do We Really Need Quantum Machine Learning?: A Multidimensional Empirical Study
authors: Sudip Vhaduri, Ryan Gammon, Sayanton Dibbo
abstract: The rapid growth of computer vision and increasingly complex image recognition tasks has exposed fundamental computational limitations of classical machine learning models, motivating the exploration of quantum computing as an emerging new paradigm. This paper presents a comprehensive benchmarking study of classical and quantum machine learning models for image recognition on the MNIST handwritten digit dataset, evaluating both traditional models, a Classical Support Vector Machine (CSVM) and a Quantum Support Vector Machine (QSVM), and deep neural network models, a Classical Convolutional Neural Network (CCNN) and a Quantum Convolutional Neural Network (QCNN), across four performance dimensions: classification accuracy, computational runtime, parameter count, and memory requirements. Experiments are conducted as functions of both feature dimensionality and sample size, and across CPU and GPU execution environments, providing a controlled, multidimensional comparison to address gaps in prior work. For the SVM-based models, QSVM consistently outperforms CSVM in accuracy, reaching $\sim$ 0.90 versus $\sim$ 0.85 at 1,000 samples, with a higher computational cost. A feature count of 10 qubits and a sample size in the range of 200 -- 500 emerge as practical operating points that balance accuracy and runtime. For the neural network models, CCNN and QCNN achieve comparable classification accuracy, both exceeding 0.96 at 64 features and 60,000 samples, yet QCNN offers substantially superior parameter and memory efficiency, requiring $\sim$ 94\% fewer parameters and $\sim$ 75\% less memory than CCNN at higher feature counts, while incurring higher runtime. Across both model families, quantum models consistently outperform classical models by greater margins in accuracy as feature dimensionality or sample size increases.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive benchmark of quantum versus classical machine learning models for image recognition. It finds that while quantum models can be computationally expensive, they offer significant advantages in parameter and memory efficiency, particularly in high-dimensional data regimes.
main_problem: Evaluating the practical utility and necessity of Quantum Machine Learning (QML) for computer vision tasks by identifying specific regimes where quantum models provide a tangible advantage over classical models.
main_result: Quantum models show greater relative accuracy advantages as feature dimensionality or sample size increases; specifically, QCNNs achieve comparable accuracy to CCNNs but with ~94% fewer parameters and ~75% less memory.
method: A multidimensional benchmarking study comparing CSVM, QSVM, CCNN, and QCNN using the MNIST dataset, evaluating performance across varying feature counts, sample sizes, and CPU/GPU environments.
model_or_system: The study uses simulated quantum hardware via the PennyLane library, implementing Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVM) with angle embedding and Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN) using variational quantum circuits with RX, RY, and CNOT gates.
key_observables: Classification accuracy, computational runtime, parameter count, and memory requirements.
important_parameters: Feature dimensionality (qubit count), sample size, and execution environment (CPU vs. GPU).
main_assumptions: The study is limited to the MNIST dataset and relies on quantum simulations rather than real hardware; it assumes PCA components equal the number of qubits for encoding.
figures_summary: Bar graphs and dual-axis plots showing accuracy and runtime variations for SVMs across qubit/feature counts and sample sizes, and performance metrics (accuracy, runtime, parameters, memory) for CNNs across varying feature counts and sample sizes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation of QML in computer vision, details the experimental setup and models (SVM and CNN variants), presents a multidimensional comparative analysis of accuracy and efficiency, and concludes with potential applications in edge computing and limitations of the study.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Bar graph plot of (a) accuracy (left) and (b) runtime (right) variation across the CSVM and QSVM with CPU and GPU when varying qubits/features but keeping sample size fixed (i.e., 300).
Fig. 2: Accuracy vs. runtime tradeoff of QSVM using GPU varying qubits/features but keeping sample size fixed (i.e., 300).
Fig. 3: Bar graph plot of (a) accuracy (left) and (b) runtime (right) variation across the CSVM and QSVM with CPU and GPU when varying sample size but keeping qubits/features fixed (i.e., 12).
Fig. 4: Effect of varying sample size but keeping qubits/features fixed (i.e., 12)

Fig. 5: Bar graph plot of (a) accuracy, (b) runtime (s), (c) total parameters, and (d) memory (kB) variation across the CCNN and QCNN with CPU and GPU at epoch 5 when varying features but keeping sample size fixed (i.e., 60000).
